questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The experimentally determined molar mass of a non-volatile solute, $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ in water by Cottrell's method, is
options: equal to the calculated molar mass
more than the calculated molar mass
less than the calculated molar mass
double of the calculated molar mass
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ being ionic in nature, ionises completely to give $\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}$ and $2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(1+2=3)$ ions, i.e., number of particles increases in the solution.
$\therefore \quad i=\frac{3}{1}=3=\frac{M_c}{M_o}$
(where, $M_c=$ calculated molar mass and $M_o=$ observed molar mass)
$\begin{array}{ll} & 3=\frac{M_c}{M_o} \\ \text { or } & M_o=\frac{M_c}{3} \\ \text { i.e., } \quad & M_o < M_c\end{array}$
or observed molecular mass of $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ is less than the calculated molecular mass.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
On the basis of information given below mark the correct option. Information:
(A) In bromoethane and chloroethane mixture intermolecular interactions of A-A and B-B type are nearly same as A-B type interactions.
(B) In ethanol and acetone mixture, A-A or B-B type intermolecular interactions are stronger than A-B type interactions.
(C) In chloroform and acetone mixture, A-A or B-B type intermolecular interactions are weaker than A-B type interactions.
options: Solution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>)</mo></math> will follow Raoult's law.
Solution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>)</mo></math> will show negative deviation from Raoult's law.
Solution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>)</mo></math> will show positive deviation from Raoult's law.
questions.solution:
Each constituent of a binary liquid solution will obey Rault's law if the A-A and B-B forces of attraction are more or less the same as the A-B force of attraction. In such a case, the total vapour pressure of the solution is the sum of the partial vapour pressures of components of the solution.

The bromoethane and chloroethane mixture follows the Raoult's law because the intermolecular interactions of A-A and B-B type are nearly the same as A-B type interactions.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

The number of moles of $\mathrm{N}_2$ from air dissolved in 10 moles of water at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ and 5 atm pressure is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following is not correct?
questions.solution: Ethanol has hydrogen bonding while acetone has dipole-dipole forces. Thus, their mixture would form a non-ideal solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The freezing point of solution containing $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of non-volatile and non-electrolyte liquid " $A$ " in $500 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is $-0.413^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If $K_f$ of water is $1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and the molecular weight of $A=60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, what is the density of the solution in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mL}^{-1}$ ? (Assume $\Delta_{\text {mix }} V=0$ )
questions.solution:
Given,
Volume of solute $=10 \mathrm{~mL}$
$\Delta T_f=0-(-0.413)=0.413^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$w($ solvent $)=500 \mathrm{~g}$
$K_f$ (water) $=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\left(=w_A\right)$
Molecular weight of $(A)$ or $M_B=60 \mathrm{~g}$
Let mass of solute $(A)=w_B$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad \Delta r_f=K_f \times \frac{w_{\mathcal{B}}}{M_{\mathcal{B}}} \times \frac{1000}{w_A} \\
& 0.413=1.86 \times \frac{w_{\mathcal{B}}}{60} \times \frac{1000}{500} \\
& \therefore \quad w_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{0.413 \times 60 \times 500}{1.86 \times 1000} \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
Mass of solute $=6.66 \mathrm{~g}$
Also,
$$
\begin{gathered}
\because \text { Density }(d) \text { of solution }=\frac{\text { Total mass }}{\text { Total volume }} \\
=\frac{6.66+500}{500+10}=\frac{506.66}{510}=0.993\left(\mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$
Density $(d)$ of solution $=0.993 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-2}$
Hence, option (4) is the correct answer.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Calculate the molal depression constant of a solution, which freezes at $15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The latent heat of fusion is $180.7 \mathrm{Jg}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
$K_f=\frac{R T_f^2}{1000 \times L_f}$
Where,
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_f & =\text { molal depression constant } \\
R & =\text { gas constant }=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
T_f & =\text { freezing point }=273+15=288 \mathrm{~K} \\
L_f & =\text { latent heat of fusion }=180.73 \mathrm{Jg}^{-1} \\
K_f & =\frac{8.314 \times(288)^2}{1000 \times 180.7}=\frac{8.314 \times 82944}{1807} \\
& =\frac{68959}{18070}=3.81 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{molal}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When $25 \mathrm{~g}$ of a non-volatile solute is dissolved in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water, the vapour pressure is lowered by $2.25 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~mm}$. If the vapour pressure of water at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $17.5 \mathrm{~mm}$, what is the molecular weight of the solute?
questions.solution:
Given,
weight of non-volatile solute,
$$
w=25 \mathrm{~g}
$$
Weight of solvent, $W=100 \mathrm{~g}$
Lowering of vapour pressure,
$$
p^{\circ}-p_s=0.225 \mathrm{~mm}
$$
Vapour pressure of pure solvent,
$$
p^{\circ}=17.5 \mathrm{~mm}
$$
Molecular weight of solvent $\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right), M=18 \mathrm{~g}$
Molecular weight of solute, $m=$ ?
According to Raoult's law
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p^{\circ}-p_s}{p^{\circ}} & =\frac{w \times M}{m \times W} \\
\frac{0.225}{17.5} & =\frac{25 \times 18}{m \times 100} \\
m & =\frac{25 \times 18 \times 17.5}{22.5} \\
& =350 \mathrm{~g}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: What will be the molar mass of solute if vapour pressure of pure benzene is $450 \mathrm{~mm}$ $\mathrm{Hg}$ when $1 \cdot 5 \mathrm{~g}$ of non volatile solute is added to $30 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzene? (Vapour pressure of solution $=400 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$, atomic mass $\mathrm{C}=12, \mathrm{H}=1$ )
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{P}^{0}=450 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~Hg},\quad\mathrm{P}=400 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~Hg}, \quad \mathrm{M}_{1}=78 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ (molar mass of benzene),
$\mathrm{W}_{1}=30 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{~W}_{2}=1.5 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{M}_{2}=?$
$\mathrm{M}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{W}_{2} \mathrm{M}_{1}}{\mathrm{~W}_{1}} \times \frac{\mathrm{P}_{0}}{\left(\mathrm{P}_{0}-\mathrm{P}\right)}$
$\therefore \mathrm{M}_{2}=\frac{1.5 \times 78}{30} \times \frac{450}{(450-400)}=35.1 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
At $293 \mathrm{~K}$, the Henry law constant in water for $\mathrm{N}_2$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$ are $76.48 \mathrm{k}$ bar and $34.86 \mathrm{k}$ bar respectively. What is the ratio of mole fractions of $\mathrm{N}_2$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$ in water?
(Assume partial pressures of $\mathrm{N}_2$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$ same at $293 \mathrm{~K}$ )
questions.solution:
According to Henry's law :-
$$
\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{xK}_{\mathrm{H}}
$$
For $\mathrm{N}_2$ :- $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_2}=\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{N}_2} \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\mathrm{N}_2\right)$
For $\mathrm{O}_2$ :- $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_2}=\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{O}_2} \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)$
We have, $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_2}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_2}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\mathrm{N}_2\right)=76.48 \mathrm{k}$ bar, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)=3486 \mathrm{k}$ bar
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{N}_2}}{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}=\frac{\left(\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_2}}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\mathrm{N}_2\right)}\right)}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)}\right)}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{N}_2}}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\mathrm{N}_2\right)} \times \frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)}{\frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{O}_2}} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\mathrm{N}_2\right)}=\frac{34.86}{76.48}=0.45\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The observed molar mass determined for $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ by freezing point depression method is $50.0 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$. The molecular weight of $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ is 142 . What will be the degree of dissociation $\alpha$ for $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ in water?
questions.solution:
van't Hoff factor, $\begin{aligned} i & =\frac{(\text { Molar mass })_{\text {calculated }}}{(\text { Molar mass })_{\text {observed }}} \\ & =\frac{142}{50}=2.84\end{aligned}$ $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ is an electrolyte in water
$\Rightarrow \quad \alpha=\frac{i-1}{n-1}=\frac{2.84}{3-1}=0.92$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The vapour pressure of water at T (K) is 20 mm Hg. The following solutions are prepared at T (K)
I. 6 g of urea (molecular weight = 60) is dissolved in 178.2 g of water.
II. 0.01 mol of glucose is dissolved in 179.82 g of water.
III. 5.3 g of Na2CO3 (molecular weight = 106) is dissolved in 179.1 g of water.
Identify the correct order in which the vapour pressure of solutions increases
questions.solution:
I. XB=nBnA+nB
=660178.218+660=0.01
ΔPP0=XB=0.01
II. XB=nBnA+nB
=0.01179.8218+0.01=0.001
ΔPP0=0.001
III. XB=nBnA+nB
=5.3106179.118+5.3106=0.005
ΔPP0=iXB
=3×0.005=0.015
So vapour pressure of solution will increase in the following sequence.
III < I < II
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>44</mn></math> grams of benzoic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> dissolved in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn></math> grams of benzene, it shows depression of freezing point equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. Molal depression constant of benzene is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is the percentage association of acid, if it forms dimer in solution?
questions.solution:
Given ,

WB=2.44g , Kf=5 K kg mol-1WA =25g , ∆Tf  =2.2K 
WB=2.44g , Kf=5 K kg mol-1WA =25g , ∆Tf  =2.2K Now ,∆Tf = Kf ×WBMB ×1000WAMB=5×2.44×10002.2×25=221.8g/mol2C6H5COOH ⇔C6H5COOH 2If x is the degree of association , (1-x) mole ofbenzoic acid left undissociated and corresponding x2as associated moles of C6H5COOH at equilibrium.Total number of moles at equilibrium 1-x+x2=1-x2i=Normal molecular massAbnormal molecular mass1-x2 =122221.8x=0.900=90%
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The vapour pressure of water at $23^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $19.8 \mathrm{~mm}, 0.1$ mole of glucose is dissolved in $178.2 \mathrm{~g}$ of water. What is the vapour pressure (in $\mathrm{mm}$ ) of the resultant solution?
questions.solution:
Given $P_s=19.8 \mathrm{~mm}$
$\begin{aligned}
& n_A=0.1 \\
& n_B=\frac{178.2}{18}=9.9
\end{aligned}$
According to Raoult's law
$\begin{gathered}
\frac{P_s-P}{P_s}=\frac{n_A}{n_A+n_B} \\
\frac{19.8-P}{19.8}=\frac{0.1}{9.9+0.1}
\end{gathered}$
or
$\begin{aligned}
198-10 P & =19.8 \times 0.1 \\
10 P & =198-1.98 \\
10 P & =196.02 \\
P & =19.602 \mathrm{~mm}
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
An aqueous solution of urea (mol. mass  60  g mol −1 ) boils at 100.18 o C at 1 atm . Find the freezing temperature of soultion.

Given: Kf and Kb for water are 1.86 and 0.512  K kg mol −1 , respectively.
questions.solution:
ΔTf=Kfm......(1)ΔTb=Kbm......(2)}⇒ΔTfΔTb=KfKb ...... (3)

ΔTf→ Depression in freezing point

ΔTb→ Elevation in boiling point

Boiling point of water = 100 o C

Kf =1.86 K  kg mol −1

Boiling point of urea in water = 100.18 o C

Kb=0.512 K  kg mol −1

⇒ΔTb=0.18

Freezing point of water = 0 o C

Freezing point of urea in water =− T o C

⇒ΔTf=T

⇒ from equation (3),

T0.18=1.860.512⇒T=0.6539

⇒ Freezing point urea in water  =−0 .654 o C
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A mixture of 3.0 moles of $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O}$ and $1.5 \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{KO}_2$ is dissolved in $1000 \mathrm{~mL}$ of water. The vapour pressure of the solution in Torr, at $100{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{KO}_2$, both are ionic compounds and are completely ionised as follows :
(i) $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{O}^{2-}(i=3)$
(ii) $\mathrm{KO}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}^{+}+2 \mathrm{O}^{2-}(i=3)$
Also,
$\because \quad \frac{p^{\circ}-p_s}{p^{\circ}}=i \cdot \chi_{\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O}}+i \cdot \chi_{\mathrm{KO}_2}$
$\text { and moles of } \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}=\frac{1000}{18}=55.5$
where, $p^{\circ}=$ atmospheric pressure
$p_s=\text { vapour pressure of solution }$
$ p^{\circ}=760$
$\therefore \frac{p^{\circ}-p_s}{p^{\circ}}=3 \times \frac{3}{3+3+55.5}+3 \times \frac{1.5}{1.5+1.5+55.5}$
$=\frac{9}{61.5}+\frac{1.5 \times 3}{58.5}=\frac{9}{61.5}+\frac{4.5}{58.5}$
$\frac{760-p_s}{760}=0.14+0.07=0.21 \approx 0.20$
$p_s=608$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: pH of a 0.1 M monobasic acid (HA) is measured to be 2. Its osmotic pressure at temperature T K is
questions.solution:
[H+]=Cα;[H+]=10-pH
So Cα=10-2
0.1 α=10-2
α=0.1
α=i-1n-1;0.1=i-12-1
i=1.1
So π=iCRT=1.1×0.1×RT=0.11RT
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: At $T(\mathrm{~K})$ the molarity of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ (in $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}$ ) in 200 $\mathrm{mL}$ of soda water packed under a pressure of 3.4 bar is $\left(K_{\mathrm{H}}\right.$ of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ in water is $1.7 \times 10^3$ bar at $T(\mathrm{~K}))$
questions.solution:
Given, volume $=200 \mathrm{~mL}$
pressure, $p=3.4 \mathrm{bar}$
$K_{\mathrm{H}}$ of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ in water $=1.7 \times 10^3$ bar
According to Henry's law, $p_{\mathrm{CO}_2}=K_{\mathrm{H}} \cdot \chi$
$\chi=\frac{p_{\mathrm{CO}_2}}{K_{\mathrm{H}}}$
$\frac{n_{\mathrm{CO}_2} \times 18}{200}=\frac{3.4}{1.7 \times 10^3}$
$n_{\mathrm{CO}_2}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 200}{18}=22.22 \times 10^{-3}$
Molarity $=\frac{\text { Number of mole of solute }}{\text { Volume }}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{22.22 \times 10^{-3}}{200 \times 10^{-3}}=0.1111 \\ & =1.11 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The elevation in boiling point, when 13.44 g of freshly prepared CuCl<sub>2</sub> are added to one kilogram of water, is. [Some useful data, k<sub>b   </sub>=  0.52 kg K mol<sup>-1</sup>, molecular weight of CuCl<sub>2</sub> = 134.4 g].
questions.solution:
CuCl 2 aq → Cu aq 2 + + 2 Cl aq -
with complete dissociation in aqueous solution.

Van't Hoff factor = 3 = i

Δ T b = i K b · m

= 3 × 0.52 × 13.44 134.4 1000 1000

Moles of ClCl2 = 1 3 · 4 4 1 3 4 · 4 = 0 · 1 mole


= 3 × 0.52 × 0.1

= 0.156 K ≈ 0.16 K
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the density of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> solution of ethylene glycol in water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>11</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>ml</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the molality (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>) of the solution is approximately
questions.solution:
Density of solution = 1.11 gml-1

Molarity of solution = 2M

Density is the amount of x g of solution in 1000ml of solution.

Mass of solution = density ×volume=1.11×1000=1110g

Molality is equal to the number of moles of solute present in 1000 g of solution.

Number of moles of solute = 2 mole

Molar mass of ethylene glycol= 62 g/mol

Mass of solute = Mole×molar mass=2×62=124 g

Mass of the solvent = 1110-124=986g

Molality=Number of moles of soluteMass of solvent in g×1000

Molality=2986×1000=2.02m

Hence, the molality of solution is 2.02 m(approx.)
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The freezing point depression of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi>y</mi></msub><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>CN</mi></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>58</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. linebreak="newline"></mspace><mo>∆</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>iK</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub><mi questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When mercuric iodide is added to the aqueous solution of potassium iodide the
questions.solution:
When mercuric iodide is added to the aqueous solution of potassium iodide the freezing point is raised as ΔTf decreases due to complex formation number of species decrease and molecular weight increases.
ΔT f ∝ 1 Molecular weight ∝ number of species
HgI2 although insoluble in water but shows complex formation with KI as
2KI +  HgI 2 → K 2 HgI 4
2KI→ 2K + +2I −  (4 ions)
K 2 HgI 4 → 2K + HgI 4 −2  (3 ions)
Here number of species decreases due to the reaction. Hence, ΔT f ↓ and T f ↑
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Correct order of osmotic pressure of the following solutions is
(i) $30 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ of glucose
(ii) $60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{NH}_2 \mathrm{CONH}_2$
(iii) $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ of glucose
(iv) $58.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{NaCl}$
questions.solution:
$\because \mathrm{NaCl}$ is an ionic salt and go for complete
ionisation, so has maximum osmotic pressure, while glucose and urea does not ionises. For $\mathrm{NaCl}(i=2)$ and for glucose and urea $(i=1)$
$\because \quad \pi=i C R T$
where, $\pi=$ osmotic pressure
$i=$ van't Hoff factor
$C=$ molarity
$R=$ gas constant
$T=$ temperature in Kelvin
Also,
$\pi=i \frac{n}{V} R T \quad\left[\because C=\frac{n}{V}\right]$
and
$\pi=i \frac{w}{M \times V} R T$
If $V, R$ and $T$ are same $\pi \propto i \times \frac{W}{M}$
where, $n=$ number of moles of solute
$\begin{aligned} W & =\text { mass of solute } \\ M & =\text { molar mass of solute } \\ V & =\text { volume }\end{aligned}$
Thus, (i) $30 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$ of glucose
$\begin{aligned}(w=30, M=180) & \\ & \pi \propto \frac{1 \times 30}{180}=0.166\end{aligned}$
(ii)
$\begin{aligned} 60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1} & \text { of } \mathrm{NH}_2 \mathrm{CONH}_2 \\ (w=60, M & =60) \\ \pi & \propto \frac{1 \times 60}{60}=1.00\end{aligned}$
(iii) $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$ of glucose
$\begin{aligned}(w=80, M & =180) \\ \pi & \propto \frac{1 \times 80}{80}=0.44\end{aligned}$
(iv) $58.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{NaCl}$
$(i=2) w=58.5, M=58.5$
$\therefore \quad \pi \propto i \frac{w}{M}=2 \times \frac{58.5}{58.5}=2$
Hence, order of osmotic pressure will be
(i) $ < $ (iii) $ < $ (ii) $ < $ (iv)
Hence, (b) is the correct answer.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The molality and molarity of a solution of a glucose in water which is labelled as $10 \%$ $(w / w)$ are respectively (density of solution $\left.=1.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
Mass by mass percentage is the mass of solute dissolved in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of the solution. Mass of solution $=100 \mathrm{~g}$ Mass of glucose $=10 \mathrm{~g}$ Mass of solvent $=90 \mathrm{~g}$ Molar mass of glucose $\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_6\right)$ $=12 \times 6+1 \times 12+16 \times 6=180 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$ $\begin{gathered}\text { Molality }=\frac{\text { Mass of solute }}{\text { Molar mass of solute }} \times \frac{1000}{\text { Mass of solvent }} \\ \text { (in g) }\end{gathered}$ $=\frac{10}{180} \times \frac{1000}{90}=0.617 \mathrm{~m}$ Also, volume of solution $=\frac{\text { Mass of solution }}{\text { Density of solution }}$
$=\frac{100 \mathrm{~g}}{1.2 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}}=83.33 \mathrm{~mL}$ $\therefore$ Molarity $=\frac{10}{180} \times \frac{1000}{83.33}=0.67 \mathrm{M}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: FeCl3 on reaction with K4[FeCN)6 in aqueous solution gives blue colour. These are separated by a semipermeable membrane AB as shown. Due to osmosis there is
options: blue colour formation in side X
blue colour formation in side Y
blue colour formation in both of the side X and Y
questions.solution: There will be no blue colour formation because in the process of osmosis, solvent molecules migrate across the membrane and not solute molecules.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A camphor sample melts at $176^{\circ} \mathrm{C} . K_f$ for camphor is $40 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. A solution of $0.02 \mathrm{~g}$ of a hydrocarbon in $0.8 \mathrm{~g}$ of camphor melts at $156.77^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The hydrocarbon is made up of $92.3 \%$ of carbon. What is the molecular formula of the hydrocarbon?
questions.solution:
Melting point of camphor $=176^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ $\left(K_f\right)$ for camphor $=40 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ Mass of hydrocarbon $\left(w_B\right)=0.02 \mathrm{~g}$ Mass of camphor $\left(w_A\right)=0.8 \mathrm{~g}$ Temperature of camphor (at which it melts) $=156.77^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ Thus, $\Delta T_f$ for camphor $=176-156.77$ $=19.23^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=19.23 \mathrm{~K}$ $\therefore$ Depression in freezing point, $\begin{aligned} \Delta T_f & =\frac{K_f \times w_B}{M_B} \times \frac{1000}{W_A} \\ 19.23 & =\frac{40 \times 0.02 \times 1000}{M_B \times 0.8}\end{aligned}$ Molar mass of solute $\left(M_B\right)$ $=\frac{40 \times 0.02 \times 1000}{19.23 \times 0.8}=52$
$\therefore$ Empirical formula $=\mathrm{CH}$ Empirical formula weight $=12+1=13$ Multiple $(n)=\frac{\text { Molecular weight }}{\text { Empirical formula weight }}=\frac{52}{13}=4$ Thus, molecular formula $=(\mathrm{CH})_4=\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_4$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Which of the following pair of solutions is isotonic?
A. $18 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$ of glucose of solution and $6 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$ of Urea solution
B. $10 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$ of glucose solution and $10 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$ of Urea solution
C. $\quad 0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$ solution and $0.02 \mathrm{M}$ glucose solution
D.  $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaCl}$ solution and $0.01 \mathrm{M}$ glucose solution
(Assume that $\mathrm{NaCl}$ undergoes complete dissociation)
questions.solution:
Osmotic pressure $\pi=\mathrm{CRT}$; $\mathrm{C}$ is in moles/L.
$\mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{MV}}$
$\mathrm{m}=$ mass of solute and $\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{mol}$. wt. of solute $\mathrm{V}=$ 
Volume in $\mathrm{L}$.
At a given $\mathrm{T}$, isotonic solution; $\pi_1=\pi_2 ; \mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{RT}=\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{RT}$
(A) $18 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$ of glucose $=(18 / 180) / \mathrm{L}=0.1 \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}=0.1(\mathrm{M})$
and $6 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$ of Urea $=(6 / 60) / \mathrm{L}=0.1 \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}=0.1(\mathrm{M})$
(B.) $10 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$ of glucose $=\left(\frac{10}{180}\right) / \mathrm{L}=(1 / 18) / \mathrm{L}=0.06(\mathrm{M})$
$10 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$ of urea $=\left(\frac{10}{60}\right) / \mathrm{L}=(1 / 6) / \mathrm{L}=0.17(\mathrm{M})$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{C}_1 \neq \mathrm{C}_2$
(C) $0.01(\mathrm{M}) \quad \mathrm{NaOH}$ solution and $0.02(\mathrm{M})$ glucose solution are isotonic.
$\begin{aligned} & \pi_{\mathrm{NaOH}}=2 \times 0.01 \times \mathrm{RT}=0.02 \mathrm{RT} \\ & \pi_{\text {glucose }}=0.02 \mathrm{RT}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \pi_{\mathrm{NaOH}}=\pi_{\text {glucose }} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{C}_1 \neq \mathrm{C}_2\end{aligned}$
(D) $0.01(\mathrm{M}) \mathrm{NaCl}$ solution are $0.01(\mathrm{M})$ glucose solution
$\begin{aligned} & \pi_{\mathrm{NaCl}}=2 \times 0.01 \times \mathrm{RT} \\ & \pi_{\text {glucose }}=0.01 \times \mathrm{R} \times \mathrm{T}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad \pi_{\mathrm{NaCl}} \neq \pi_{\text {glucose }}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A beaker containing a mixture of liquid \(A\) and liquid \(B\), in equilibrium with each other, is cooled very slowly. If point ' \(D\) ' on the graph is where solidification of both liquids is complete, how many different phases co-exist along line ' \(C D\) ' if freezing point of liquid \(B\) and liquid \(A\) are \(-25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) and \(-78^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) respectively.
questions.solution: At point ' \(D\) ', solidification of both liquids, \(A\) and \(B\) takes place. So, along the line ' \(C D\) ' on the graph four phases will co-exist, which are liquid- \(A\), solid- \(A\), liquid- \(B\) and solid- \(B\).
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: At \(298 \mathrm{~K}\), the vapour pressure of a solution of \(7.5 \mathrm{~g}\) of non-volatile solute in \(90 \mathrm{~g}\) of water is \(2.8 \mathrm{kPa}\). If \(18 \mathrm{~g}\) of water is added to this solution the vapour pressure becomes \(2.81 \mathrm{kPa}\) at same temperature, the molar mass of solute in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\) is
questions.solution:
Key Idea Relation between relative lowering of vapour pressure and molecular mass of solute is given by
\(\frac{p^{\circ}-p}{p^{\circ}}=\frac{w_2 / M_2}{w_1 M_1}\)
Given,
Weight of non-volatile solute, \(w_2=7.5 \mathrm{~g}\)
Weight of water, \(w_1=90 \mathrm{~g}\)
Vapour pressure of solution \(=2.8 \mathrm{kPa}\)
In first case,
\(\begin{aligned}
\frac{p^{\circ}-2.8}{p^{\circ}} & =\frac{7.5 / M_2}{90 / 18} \\
\frac{p^{\circ}-2.8}{p^{\circ}} & =\frac{1.5}{M_2} \quad \ldots (i)
\end{aligned}\)
In second case,
\(\frac{p^{\circ}-2.81}{p^{\circ}}=\frac{7.5 / M_2}{108 / 18}=\frac{1.25}{M_2} \quad \ldots (ii)\)
On solving (i) and (ii) we get
\(M_2=71.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\)
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: At $298 \mathrm{~K}$, the vapour pressure of pure water is 25 torr. The vapour pressure of water, when $12 \mathrm{~g}$ of urea (molar mass, $60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) and $36 \mathrm{~g}$ of glucose (molar mass, $180 \mathrm{~g}$ $\mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) is dissolved in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water at the sametemperature (in torr) is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}^{\circ}=25$ torr; $\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{u}}=12 / 60=0.2$ moles;
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=36 / 180=0.2 \text { moles; } \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}=100 / 18=5.55 \text { moles } \\ & \therefore \chi_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}=\frac{5.55}{5.55+0.2+0.2}=\frac{5.55}{5.95} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}=\chi_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}} \mathrm{P}^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}=\frac{5.55}{5.95} \times 25=23.32\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of $2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$ solution of monoacidic weak base if it ionizes to the extent of $5 \%$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\alpha \mathrm{c}} \\ & \mathrm{C}=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M} \\ & \alpha=0.05 \\ & {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.05} \\ & =1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M} \\ & \mathrm{pH}=4\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: An aqueous solution of hydrochloric acid -
options: Shows negative deviations from Raoults’ law
Shows positive deviations from Raoults’ law
Obeys Henrys’ law at all compositions
questions.solution: It shows negative deviation from Raoult’s law. If the attraction between different molecules (for example, between HCl and H<sub>2</sub>O molecules) is stronger, the escaping tendency from the solution to the vapour phase will be smaller. As a result the partial vapour pressure will be smaller than predicted by Raoult’s law and the system exhibits a negative deviation.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If aqueous solution contains \(9 \%\) and \(1 \%\) \((w / w)\) of two non-volatile non-electrolytes \(X\) (molecular weight 180) and \(Y\) (molecular weight 50) respectively, the boiling point of solution in \({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) approximately is \(\left(K_b=0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)\)
questions.solution:
Given,
Mass of non-volatile solute \(X=9 \mathrm{~g}\)
Mass of non-volatile solute \(Y=\mathbf{l} \mathbf{g}\)
Molar mass of \(X=180 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\)
Molar mass of \(Y=50 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\)
Mass of solvent (approx.) \(=100\)
\(\begin{aligned}
K_b & =0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
\Delta T_b & =K_b \times M=K_b\left[M_{(X)}+M_{(Y)}\right] \\
& =K_b\left[\frac{w_{(X)}}{M_{(X)}}+\frac{w_{(Y)}}{M_{(Y)}}\right] \times \frac{1000}{100} \\
& =0.52\left[\frac{9}{180}+\frac{1}{50}\right] 10 \\
& =0.52[0.05+0.02] 10 \\
& =0.52[0.07] 10=0.36
\end{aligned}\)
Thus, boiling point \(=100+0.36\)
\(=100.36 \text { or } 100.4\)
Hence, option (b) is the correct answer.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The variation of vapour pressure $(b)$ as a function of temperature $(a)$ is studied for $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OC}_2 \mathrm{H}_5, \mathrm{CCl}_4$ and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ at $760 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$ and is shown in the figure below. The boiling temperatures of $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OC}_2 \mathrm{H}_5, \mathrm{CCl}_4$ and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ are 308,350 and $373 \mathrm{~K}$ respectively. Curves $A, B, C$ respectively correspond to
questions.solution:
Given, Boiling point (bp) at $760 \mathrm{~mm}$ of $\mathrm{Hg}$ for (i) $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OC}_2 \mathrm{H}_5=308 \mathrm{~K}$ (ii) $\mathrm{CCl}_4=350 \mathrm{~K}$ (iii) $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}=373 \mathrm{~K}$ $\because$ According to definition, the boiling point of a liquid is that temperature at which its vapour pressure becomes equal to the atmospheric pressure. Now, weaker is the attractive force between the molecules of given species, more is its volatile nature and smaller is its boiling point, i.e. attractive force $\propto \frac{1}{\text { volatile }}$ nature between the molecules, volatile nature $\propto$ vapour pressure over the liquid. Temperature for boiling point, $\propto \frac{1}{\text { volatile }}$ nature. Now, drawing perpendicular on $x$-axis, i.e. on (a) from points $B, A$ and $C$, we have $$ T_C>T_A>T_B . $$
Hence, $$ \begin{aligned} & (A)=\mathrm{CCl}_4 \\ & (B)=\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OC}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \\ & (C)=\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \end{aligned} $$ Thus, option (c) is the correct answer.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Calculate the approximate $\Delta T_b$ (in K) for 0.001 molal $\mathrm{KCl}$ solution, if its van't-Hoff factor is $1.98\left[K_b\right.$ of water is $\left.0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
questions.solution:
We know that
$$
\Delta T_b=i K_b m \quad\left(\because \Delta T_b=i . K_b \times m\right)
$$
where, $\Delta T_b=$ elivation in boiling point (to time)
$$
\begin{aligned}
i & =\text { van't-Hoff factor }(1.98) \\
K_b & =\text { molal elevation constant }(0.52) \\
m & =\text { molality }(0.001 \mathrm{~m}) \\
\Delta T_b & =1.98 \times 0.52 \times 0.001=0.0010296 \\
\Delta T_b & =1.03 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
$1.8 \mathrm{~g}$ of glucose (molar mass $180 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) is dissolved in $0.1 \mathrm{~kg}$ of water. The freezing point of the solution ( $\mathrm{in}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) is
$\left(K_f\right.$ for water $\left.=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
Depression in freezing point is given as $\Delta T=i K_f \times m$ where, $i=$ van't Hoff factor $\begin{gathered}K_f=\text { molal freezing point depression } \\ \text { constant }\end{gathered}$ $m=$ molality Molar mass of glucose $\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_6\right)$ $=12 \times 6+12 \times 1+6 \times 16=180 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$
$=\frac{1.8}{180} \times \frac{1}{0.1}=0.1 \mathrm{~m}$ $\therefore \Delta T=\mathrm{I} \times 1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{mol} \times 0.1 \mathrm{~m}$ $=0.186^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ $\therefore$ Freezing point of solution $=$ Freezing point of water - Depression in freezing point $=0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-0.186^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=-0.186^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For which of the following liquid mixtures $\Delta_{\text {mix }} H=0$ and $\Delta_{\text {mix }} V=0$ ?
questions.solution: ( Ideal solutions, are possible if molecules of solute and solvent are almost of same size and have identical polarity and they should have almost same inter molecular attraction. For example, solutions of ethyl chloride and ethyl bromide almost behave as ideal solutions. The characteristics of ideal solution is that no heat should be evolved or absorbed during the mixing, i.e. $\Delta H_{\text {mix }}=0$ and also no expansion or contraction on mixing, i.e. $\Delta V_{\text {mix }}=0$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: \(6 \mathrm{~g}\) of a mixture of naphthalene \(\left(\mathrm{C}_{10} \mathrm{H}_8\right)\) and anthracene \(\left(\mathrm{C}_{14} \mathrm{H}_{10}\right)\) is dissolved in 300 gram of benzene. If the depression in freezing point is \(0.70 \mathrm{~K}\), the composition of naphthalene and anthracene in the mixture respectively in \(\mathrm{g}\) are (molal depression constant of benzene is \(5.1 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\) )

questions.q: When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of a non-volatile, non-electrolytic solute have the empirical formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> is dissolved in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of water, the solution freezes at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>93</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The molecular formula of the solute is (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub></math> of water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>86</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
questions.solution:
Given,

Mass of the solute =36 g

Mass of the solvent =1.2 kg

∆Tf= Depression in freezing point =0.93°C

KfH2O=1.86 K kg mol-1

We know that,

∆Tf=Kf×m

Molality, m=Mass of soluteMolar mass of solute×mass of solvent kg

⇒m=36M×1.2

Substituting the values, we get,

⇒0.93=1.86×36M×1.2⇒M=60

Given,

Empirical formula =CH2O

Empirical Mass =30

So, 

n=6030=2

Hence, the molecular formula  =2CH2O=C2H4O2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: 5 g sulphur is present in 100 g of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow></msub></math> of solution 0.954 and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow></msub></math> is 4.88. The molecular formula of sulphur is
questions.solution:
ΔTb=Kb×m

m=ΔTbKb
or W2×1000M2×W1=ΔTbKb
or 5×1000M2×100=0.9544.88
M2=256
Atomic weight of sulphur = 32
Number of sulphur atom =Molecular weightAtomic weight=25632=8
Hence Molecular formula =S8 .
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The vapour pressure in $\mathrm{mm}$ of $\mathrm{Hg}$, of an aqueous solution obtained by adding $18 \mathrm{~g}$ of glucose $\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_6\right)$ to $180 \mathrm{~g}$ of water at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
questions.solution:
According to Raoult's law
$$
\frac{p^{\circ}-p_s}{p^{\circ}}=\frac{n_2}{n_1+n_2}
$$
where, $p^{\circ}=$ vapour pressure of pure water at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ $=760 \mathrm{mmHg}$.
$p_s=$ vapour pressure of solution at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& n_2=\text { moles of solute }=\frac{W_2}{M_2}=\frac{18}{180}=0.1 \mathrm{~mol} \\
& n_1=\text { moles of solvent }=\frac{W_1}{M_1}=\frac{180}{18}=10 \mathrm{~mol}
\end{aligned}
$$
By putting these values in the formula
$$
\frac{p^{\circ}-p_s}{p^{\circ}}=\frac{0.1}{10+0.1}
$$
or
$$
\begin{aligned}
10.1\left(p^{\circ}-p_s\right) & =0.1 p^{\circ} \\
10 p^{\circ} & =10.1 p_s \\
p_s=\frac{10 \times 760}{10.1} & =752.4 \mathrm{mmHg} .
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
